coredata.dc:title: Dual-response fluorescent probe for simultaneously detecting HOCl and Zn2+ in environmental water samples and evaluating the oxidative stress in the zebrafishes induced by pollutants via fluorescence imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Meng
Tong, Changlun
coredata.dc:description: Both of hypochlorous acid (HOCl) and Zn2+ ions play important roles in fundamental biological processes. They not only simultaneously exist in the biological organisms, but also coexist in the environment. Thus, developing a novel method for simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+ is very necessary. Herein, the nitrogen- and sulfur-codoped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized through a simple and rapid microwave method, it emits dual-emission fluorescence centered at 452 nm and 685 nm, which has specific recognition to HOCl and Zn2+, respectively, and they do not interfere with each other. A dual-emission and dual-response fluorescent probe was thus developed for the simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+ in the environment and fluorescence imaging in the zebrafishes. The limits of detection for HOCl and Zn2+ are 27.0 nM and 13.0 nM, respectively. It has been successfully applied for the simultaneous determination of HOCl and Zn2+ in real water samples. The sensing mechanisms of the N,S-CDs to HOCl and Zn2+ were also discussed in detail. Moreover, the fluorescent probe not only can be used to real-time dynamic monitoring of HOCl and Zn2+ in the zebrafishes, but also can be applied to evaluate the oxidative stress in the zebrafishes induced by pollutions via fluorescence imaging. This work provided a new strategy for developing multifunctional fluorescent probes, and a potential application prospect for environmental monitoring and evaluating the health risks of environmental pollutants to organisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hypochlorous acid
Zinc ion
Simultaneous determination
Fluorescence imaging
Oxidative stress

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+
Evaluating the oxidative stress induced by pollutants in the zebrafishes via fluorescence imaging
Sensing mechanism for the simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+
Analytical performance of HOCl and Zn2+
Analytical application in real environmental water samples
Cytotoxicity and fluorescence imaging of the N,S-CDs in Vivo
Probing the HOCl Levels in the zebrafishes under the exposure of Cr2O7 2- and TBBP-A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(A) The excitation and emission spectra of the N,S-CDs, (B) Fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of of HOCl or Zn2+, (C) Fluorescence lifetime curve of the N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of HOCl, (D) The Stern-Volmer plot of F
0
/F against the concentrations of HOCl.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs upon the addition of HOCl, (B) Linear plots of (F
0
-F)/F
0 versus the concentrations of HOCl, (C) Fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs upon the addition of Zn2+, (D) Linear plots of F
653
/F
685 versus the concentrations of Zn2+. F
0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs at 452 nm in the absence and presence of HOCl, respectively.
Fluorescence imaging (A) and mean fluorescence intensity (B) of exogenous and endogenous Zn2+ and HOCl in the zebrafishes.
Fluorescence imaging of HOCl in the zebrafishes under the exposure of Cr2O7 2- (A) and TBBP-A (C), mean fluorescence intensity of the blue channels for Cr2O7 2- (B) and TBBP-A (D).
Schematic illustration of the strategy for the simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+ and the application of fluorescent imaging based on the N,S-CDs.

head.ce:title: Dual-response fluorescent probe for simultaneously detecting HOCl and Zn2+ in environmental water samples and evaluating the oxidative stress in the zebrafishes induced by pollutants via fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmental Remediation and Ecological Health, Ministry of Education, College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310058, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmental Remediation and Ecological Health, Ministry of Education, College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Zhejiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Environmental Remediation and Ecological Health, Ministry of Education, College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, 310058, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Both of hypochlorous acid (HOCl) and Zn2+ ions play important roles in fundamental biological processes. They not only simultaneously exist in the biological organisms, but also coexist in the environment. Thus, developing a novel method for simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+ is very necessary. Herein, the nitrogen- and sulfur-codoped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized through a simple and rapid microwave method, it emits dual-emission fluorescence centered at 452 nm and 685 nm, which has specific recognition to HOCl and Zn2+, respectively, and they do not interfere with each other. A dual-emission and dual-response fluorescent probe was thus developed for the simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+ in the environment and fluorescence imaging in the zebrafishes. The limits of detection for HOCl and Zn2+ are 27.0 nM and 13.0 nM, respectively. It has been successfully applied for the simultaneous determination of HOCl and Zn2+ in real water samples. The sensing mechanisms of the N,S-CDs to HOCl and Zn2+ were also discussed in detail. Moreover, the fluorescent probe not only can be used to real-time dynamic monitoring of HOCl and Zn2+ in the zebrafishes, but also can be applied to evaluate the oxidative stress in the zebrafishes induced by pollutions via fluorescence imaging. This work provided a new strategy for developing multifunctional fluorescent probes, and a potential application prospect for environmental monitoring and evaluating the health risks of environmental pollutants to organisms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Both the normal oxidative metabolism and oxidative stress in the organisms can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS). The most important ROS include hydroxyl radicals (·OH), hypochlorous acid (HOCl/ClO-), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), singlet oxygen (1O2), and superoxide anion radicals (·O2 -) [1–5]. In the biological organisms, endogenous HOCl/ClO- is generated by H2O2 and chloride ions (Cl-) through the myeloperoxidase (MPO) catalytic reaction. The pathology concentration of endogenous HOCl should be less than 200 μM [6], and abnormal levels of endogenous HOCl/ClO- are associated with some diseases such as cardiovascular disease, lung injury, atherosclerosis, arthritis and cancer [7,8]. Hypochlorous acid as a powerful antimicrobial agent, has been extensively used in our daily life such as in water disinfection, blanching and other manufacturing processes, which are closely linked to our food chain and threaten to human health [9]. Additionally, HOCl was deﬁned as the third type of carcinogens issued by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) in 2017 [10]. Zn2+ ions, as the second most abundant transition-metal ions in human body, play important roles in fundamental biological processes such as cell metabolism, signal transduction, gene expression, and neurotransmission [11]. The abnormal level of zinc ions is closely related to some serious neurological diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, and hypoxic ischemia [12]. Although zinc ions are a relatively nontoxic element, it can be toxic when consumed in large enough quantities. In the environment, Zn2+ is also a metal pollutant, when the concentration attains a certain level, it may reduce the soil microbial activity and causes phytotoxic effects. In addition, HOCl as an important kind of ROS is an indicator of oxidative stress, and Zn2+ is released from macromolecules like the metallothionein during oxidative stress so that the concentration of its labile state elevates [13], so both HOCl and Zn2+ play significant roles in various biological processes dominated by oxidative stress in the organisms. Furthermore, disrupting homeostasis of Zn2+ and the abnormal concentrations of HOCl in the central nervous system are involved in the pathogenesis of many neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's diseases [14]. HOCl and Zn2+ not only simultaneously exist in the biological organisms, but also usually coexist in the environment. Thus, simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+ in the environment and biological imaging in the organisms are quite important.

Nowadays, various analytical techniques have been developed for detecting HOCl or Zn2+, among these methods fluorescent probe is generally regarded as a more powerful technique owing to its simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity. Until now, many HOCl or Zn2+ fluorescent probes have been established, most of which are organic fluorescent materials using rhodamine, coumarin, anthocyanins and BODIPY as the fluorophores, but at the same time the problems of complex synthesis, low yield, poor water solubility, high toxicity and single emission peak of organic fluorescent probes are unavoidable [15–19]. Especially, single-emission fluorescent probes are not only affected by external conditions, but also difficult to simultaneously identify multiple targets in the complex samples, which limits the use of probes in the actual application. Hence, more and more attentions have been paid to the construction of fluorescent chemical/biological ratio sensors with dual-emission peaks and dual-response sites due to the advantages of multiple objects’ determination and the visualization detection based on the obvious changes in the fluorescence colors [20,21]. It is worth noting that carbon dots (CDs) as a novel nanomaterial have the advantages of easy fabrication, low-cost, high-water solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, the unique optical and chemical stability [22,23]. The synthesis of multifunctional fluorescent CDs has attracted great attention in the fields of biological imaging, drug delivery and disease treatment [24,25]. However, most reported CDs concentrate on the single-emission and blue- or green-emitting fluorescent probes, while CDs used as the dual-emission or red-emitting fluorescent probes are seldom, especially CDs used as the dual-response fluorescent probes for simultaneously detecting multiple analytes are very seldom. As we know, no reported analytical technique can accomplish the simultaneous detection of the two important analytes of HOCl and Zn2+ especially fluorescent probe.

Considering the rich functional groups on the surface of CDs, which is easy to modify and create multiple recognition sites, our object is to fabricate a dual-emission and dual-response fluorescent probe based on the CDs and realize the simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+. According to the previously reported literature [26], glutathione (GSH) dissolved in formamide was used as raw material to prepare the CDs, which can emit the red fluorescence at 685 nm. In this work, GSH dissolved in formamide and triethylenetetramine (TETA) were selected as raw materials to prepare the nitrogen- and sulfur-codoped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) by an extremely simple, and rapid microwave method. The schematic illustration of the strategy for the simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+ and the application of fluorescent imaging based on the N,S-CDs was shown in Scheme 1. The synthesized N,S-CDs have dual-emission peaks at 452 nm and 685 nm. Interestingly, it was found that the dual-emission fluorescent probe of the N,S-CDs has selective recognition to HOCl and Zn2+, HOCl can quench the fluorescence at 452 nm. While Zn2+ ions can quench the fluorescence at 685 nm, and a new fluorescence peak at 653 nm appears, which can construct a ratiometric probe for detecting Zn2+. Moreover, the fluorescent responses of the N,S-CDs to HOCl and Zn2+ do not interfere with each other, a dual-emission and dual-response fluorescent probe was thus fabricated for simultaneously detecting HOCl and Zn2+. The probe has highly sensitive and selective for HOCl and Zn2+, and the limits of detection (LOD, 3σ/K) for HOCl and Zn2+ are 27.0 nM and 13.0 nM, respectively. It has been successfully applied for the simultaneous determination of HOCl and Zn2+ in the actual environmental water samples. The sensing mechanisms of the N,S-CDs to HOCl and Zn2+ were also discussed in detail. More importantly, the fluorescent probe of the N,S-CDs not only can be used to perform fluorescence imaging of endogenous and exogenous HOCl and Zn2+ in the zebrafishes, but also can be applied to evaluate the oxidative stress in the zebrafishes induced by pollutants via fluorescence imaging. This work provides a new strategy for developing multifunctional fluorescence probes or sensors, and a potential application prospect for simultaneously monitoring of HOCl and Zn2+ in the environmental water samples, real-time dynamic monitoring of HOCl and Zn2+ and evaluating the oxidative stress in the zebrafishes induced by pollutants via fluorescent imaging.

Reagents and instruments, synthesis of the N,S-CDs, sample collection and pretreatment, cell viability assay, and fluorescence imaging of exogenous and endogenous HOCl and Zn2+ in the zebrafishes can be found in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+
ce:section.ce:para: In a general procedure, 200 μL PBS buffer (200 mM, pH=5.5) and 30 μL of 1.0 g/L N,S-CDs solution were successively added to a 2.5 mL test tube. Then, a known concentration of HOCl (0.1–12.0 μM) or Zn2+ (0.05–3.0 μM) or simple pretreated water samples was added, the mixture solution was finally diluted to 2.0 mL, mixed completely and incubated for 10 min at 37°C. At the excitation wavelength of 385 nm, the fluorescence intensity was measured at the emission wavelengths of 452 nm, 653 nm and 685 nm. The slits were ﬁxed at 5 nm for both excitation and emission. For the selectivity test, possible coexisting interferential metal ions, anions and other active species were added to replace HOCl or Zn2+, and the same assay procedure was conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the oxidative stress induced by pollutants in the zebrafishes via fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the oxidative stress induced by pollutants, two typical pollutants such as heavy metal ions (Cr2O7 2-) and organic substance (tetrabromobisphenol A) were selected for the zebrafish exposure experiments. The zebrafishes were respectively incubated for 24 h under two typical pollutants with different concentrations including Cr2O7 2- (0, 1.0 μM, 10.0 μM and 100.0 μM) and tetrabromobisphenol A (0, 0.02 μM, 0.1 μM and 0.8 μM). After the medium was removed and washed twice with PBS buffer, 150.0 mg/L N,S-CDs solution was then added to each well and further cultured at 28.5 °C for 1 h. The zebrafishes were observed by using an inverted fluorescence microscope after the medium was removed and washed twice with PBS buffer.
Characterization and luminescent properties of the N,S-CDs, and optimization of the detection conditions can be found in Supporting Information .

The N,S-CDs were rapidly synthesized by a one-step microwave method using GSH and TETA as nitrogen/sulfur sources in the formamide solvent (Scheme S1). It is worth noting that in the preparation of the N,S-CDs, triethylenetetramine has an important effect on the luminescent properties of the N,S-CDs. As shown in Figure S1, the N,S-CDs prepared without TETA almost cannot emit the fluorescence at 452 nm when excited at 385 nm, which is consistent with the reported literature [26]. It can be explained that the microwave method decreases the size of carbonized carbon dots [27], and in the course of dialysis treatment the blue-emitting carbon dots was removed, for the mixture solution of carbon dots can emit the blue fluorescence at 452 nm before the dialysis treatment. The more interesting is that without the dialysis treatment the mixture solution of carbon dots has no fluorescence response to HOCl. While in the preparation process of the N,S-CDs, TETA was added, the obtained N,S-CDs exhibited the dual-emission peaks at 452 nm and 685 nm. The addition of TETA, not only the nitrogen doping can improve the luminescence yield and water solubility of the carbon dots [28], but also can result in abundant amino groups on the surface of the carbon dots, which promotes the electrostatic or π-π conjugated interaction between the porphyrin and the carbon dots [29], and makes the blue-emitting carbon dots to be retained after the dialysis treatment. Actually, the N,S-CDs fluorescent probe is not a simple carbon dots, while a composite probe consists of the N,S-CDs and porphyrin compounds that absorbed on the surface of the N,S-CDs [30]. In addition, in order to improve the luminescent performance of the N,S-CDs and the detection performance for Zn2+ and HOCl, the synthesis conditions of the N,S-CDs were optimized. In the synthesis process of the N,S-CDs, the solvent and the molar ratios of GSH/TETA have great effects on the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs and quenching efficiency caused by Zn2+ and HOCl. As shown in Fig. S2, 1% water in formamide as the solvent has a strong quenching effect on the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs at 452 nm, and with the increasing of water contents, both the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs and the quenching efficiency caused by Zn2+ and HOCl decrease, so pure formamide as the synthesis solvent was selected. As shown in Figure S3 , when the molar ratio of GSH/TETA is 1:5.2, the N,S-CDs emit strong fluorescence at 452 nm and 685 nm and have high quenching efficiency caused by Zn2+ and HOCl, so the molar ratio (GSH/TETA) of 1:5.2 was selected for the synthesis of the N,S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for the simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+
ce:section.ce:para: Schematic illustration of the strategy for the simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+ based on the N,S-CDs was displayed in Scheme 1. Fig. 1 B shows the changes in the fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs upon the addition of HOCl and Zn2+. It is an interesting finding that the dual-emission probe has a different fluorescence response to the two analytes of HOCl and Zn2+, and they do not interfere each other. HOCl only quenches the fluorescence at 452 nm, while Zn2+ can produce a new fluorescence peak at 653 nm and no response to the fluorescence at 452, suggesting that it is possible for the simultaneous detection of HOCl and Zn2+. Moreover, with the concentration of Zn2+ increasing, the fluorescence at 653 nm is enhanced and the fluorescence at 685 nm is quenched, which amplifies the ratiometric response signal and can improve the sensitivity of the Zn2+ detection, indicating that a self-ratiometric fluorescent probe can be fabricated for the detection of Zn2+.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N,S-CDs caused by HOCl was investigated in detail. Fig. 1 C shows that the fluorescence lifetimes of the N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of HOCl are 4.46 ns and 4.26 ns, respectively. The negligible change of fluorescence lifetimes indicates that the quenching mechanism of the N,S-CDs caused by HOCl is static quenching. The conclusion can be supported by the UV-Vis absorption spectra. Figure S4D shows that HOCl can make the absorption peak of the N,S-CDs weakened. However, as shown in Fig. 1 D, the Stern-Volmer (SV) plot of F
0
/F at 452 nm against the concentrations of HOCl is not a straight line. According to the SV equation, no matter the static quenching or dynamic quenching, the plot of F
0
/F (or τ0/τ) versus the concentrations of the quencher (Q) should be a straight line, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N,S-CDs caused by HOCl is a joint of static and dynamic quenching. The values of F
0
/F and τ0/τ response to HOCl concentrations are unequal, the value of F
0
/F (1.93) is much larger than τ0/τ (1.05) in the presence of 8.0 μM HOCl, indicating that the static quenching is a predominant mechanism [21]. Meanwhile, the sensing mechanism of the N,S-CDs to HOCl was further investigated. It can be seen from the FT-IR spectra (Figure S4C, orange line) that many characteristic peaks of the N,S-CDs have no significant change after the addition of HOCl, while the absorption peak at 1303 cm−1 increases and the absorption peak at 1231 cm−1 shifts to 1240 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of O-H/C-N and C-N, respectively. Thus, we supposed that the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs caused by HOCl does not result from the simple oxidation. Generally, according to the previously reported literatures, the fluorescent probes for sensing hypochlorite are mainly based on the two mechanisms. One is the formation of hydrogen bonds (N−H··O−Cl) between ClO- and -NH2, which constructs a bridge and cause the energy transfer from N,S-CDs to HClO [31,32], and another is to produce a non-fluorescent substance through the oxidation reaction between ClO- and the amino groups on the surface of the N,S-CDs [19]. To further confirm the above conclusion that the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs caused by HOCl does not result from the oxidation reaction, the other oxidants that possess more oxidation capacity than HOCl such as H2O2, MnO4 -, Cr2O7 2-, ClO4 -, and S2O8 2- were employed to replace HOCl. As shown in Figure S6A, even if the concentrations of other oxidants are 10 times higher than HOCl, other oxidants except for KMnO4 and K2Cr2O7 almost do not cause the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs. KMnO4 and K2Cr2O7 cause a little fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs, which only results from the inner filter effect, for these exists the overlap between UV−Vis spectra of KMnO4 or K2Cr2O7 and the fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs (see Figure S6B). Therefore, the oxidation mechanism that the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs caused by HOCl can be excluded. The formation of hydrogen bonds (N−H··O−Cl) between ClO- and -NH2, which constructs a bridge and cause the energy transfer from N,S-CDs to HClO, is the main sensing mechanism that cause the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs.

As to the sensing mechanism of the N,S-CDs to Zn2+, according to the above discussion, the fluorescence at 685 nm results from the porphyrin compounds that absorbed on the surface of the N,S-CDs. When the addition of Zn2+, a new fluorescence peak at 653 nm is appeared and the fluorescence at 685 nm is quenched. As shown in Figure S4D, with the addition of Zn2+, which can enhance the two absorption peaks of the N,S-CDs at the wavelengths of 365–420 nm. While the two weak absorption peaks at the wavelengths of 615–700 nm disappear and a strong absorption peak centred at 635 nm appears, suggesting that Zn2+ might coordinate with the porphyrin compounds, which results in a new fluorescence peak appearing at 653 nm and the fluorescence quenching at 685 nm. The FT-IR spectra of the N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs-Zn2+ can further confirm the deduction. As shown in Figure S4C (blue curve), the bending vibration peak of C-N from amide II band corresponding to 1540 cm−1 disappears in the presence of Zn2+, and the characteristic peak of CN corresponding to 1600 cm−1 increases significantly and shifts slightly to 1596 cm−1, which is probably owing to the conversion from NH-CO to NC-OH and the strong coordination between Zn2+ and N atoms [33]. In addition, the characteristic peak of -COOH on the porphyrin shifts from 1390 cm−1 to 1367 cm−1, indicating that Zn2+ can also coordinate with carboxyl groups simultaneously. The above conclusions are consistent with the UV-Vis spectra, namely, the shape and intensity of the absorption peak at 360–420 nm change dramatically and the new absorption peak at 635 nm appears, which attributes to the generation of the metal porphyrin complexes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of HOCl and Zn2+
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal conditions, the analytical performance of the dual-response fluorescent probe for HOCl and Zn2+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 A, with the addition of HOCl, the fluorescence intensity at 452 nm gradually decreases, while the fluorescence intensity at 685 nm remains unchanged. Fig. 2 B shows the linear relationship between the quenching efficiencies of (F
0
-F)/F
0 and the concentrations of HOCl in the range of 0.1−12.0 μM, the regression equation is (F
0
-F)/F
0 = 0.05149 c + 0.08597 (R2=0.9950), and the limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/K) is 27.0 nM (1.42 μg/L), which is far lower than the residual chlorine (0.6 mg/L) of disinfectant regulated by WHO [34] and the residual threshold of free chlorine from terminal pipe network (0.05 mg/L) issued by the domestic drinking water quality standard in China [35]. Furthermore, the sensitivity of the proposed probe for HOCl is superior than most of the reported fluorescence probes (Table S1). Similarly, for the quantitative detection of Zn2+, as shown in Fig. 2 C and D, the linear relationship between the F
653
/F
685 and the concentrations of Zn2+ is in the range of 0.05−3.0 μM, the regression equation is F
653
/F
685 = 0.7635 c + 0.1966 (R2=0.9953), and the limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/K) is 13.0 nM (0.85 μg/L), which meets the requirements of environmental monitoring of Zn2+, 0.05 mg/L for class I of the surface water and 1.0 mg/L for drinking water quality standard in China [36]. The sensitivity of the proposed probe for Zn2+ is superior than most of the reported fluorescence probes (Table S2). In addition, the dual-response fluorescent probe exhibits the fluorescent color changes from purple to red with the increasing concentrations of HOCl and Zn2+, which can be identified with the naked eye using a portable UV lamp. Therefore, the probe can also be used for the semi-quantitative detection of HOCl and Zn2+ in the on-site or outdoor conditions.

The good selectivity and anti-interference ability for fluorescence sensors or probes are very important in the actual applications. The selectivity of the N,S-CDs probe for HOCl and Zn2+ was evaluated in the presence of possible coexisting interferential substances including common metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cd2+), anions (Cl-, CO3 2-, NO3 -, F-, I-, HSO3 -, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, NO2 -) and various active substances (AA, GSH, Cys, Glu, O2 1, O2 •-, H2O2, NO·, ONOO-, ·OH). As shown in Figure S9, although these possible coexisting interferential substances, their concentrations are 25 or 50 times higher than the analytes, they almost have no effect on the fluorescence intensities of the sensing system. Moreover, when they coexist with HOCl or Zn2+, and still do not interfere with the determination of HOCl or Zn2+, suggesting that the N,S-CDs probe has good selectivity and anti-interference ability. The good selectivity of the N,S-CDs probe to HOCl results from the selective recognition via the unique hydrogen bond with N-H, which makes the N,S-CDs probe with a specific quenching ability to HOCl. For the good selectivity of the probe to Zn2+, it can be explained that Zn2+ might coordinate with the porphyrin compounds, which results in a new fluorescence peak appearing at 653 nm and the fluorescence quenching at 685 nm, and produces a specific ratiometric fluorescence response. While other metal ions cannot produce the specific ratiometric fluorescence response, even if some of them can quench the fluorescence at 685, they still do not interfere with the determination of Zn2+. The good selectivity and anti-interference ability of the dual-response fluorescent probe can provide a potential application prospect for simultaneously monitoring of HOCl and Zn2+ in environmental water samples and real-time dynamic monitoring of HOCl and Zn2+ in vivo via fluorescent imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application in real environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the performance of the proposed method in the practical application, six real environmental water samples including tap water, lake water, river water and swimming pool (SP) water were collected and detected by the proposed method. To check the accuracy of the proposed method, these water samples simultaneously measured by the standard methods such as N,N-diethyl-1,4-phenylenediamine (DPD) colorimetric method for HOCl and atomic absorption spectrum (AAS) method for Zn2+. As shown in Table S3, HOCl and Zn2+ could be simultaneously detected in the environmental water samples. The results also indicate that the concentrations of HOCl and Zn2+ detected by the proposed method are in good agreement with those measured by the standard methods, suggesting that the proposed method is an effective and reliable tool for simultaneously detecting HOCl and Zn2+ in the real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and fluorescence imaging of the N,S-CDs in Vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the N,S-CDs to Hela cells was evaluated using the cell proliferation toxicity test (cck-8). As shown in Figure S10, after 24 h exposure at the N,S-CDs concentrations of 0∼ 15.0 mg/L, the viability of Hela cells can still reach more than 90%, indicating that the low-toxicity N,S-CDs have some potential in the biological application. The probe concentration of 15.0 mg/L was chosen for subsequent fluorescent imaging. Considering the excellent results for the detection of HOCl and Zn2+, its imaging ability in vivo was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 A a2 and a3, the zebrafishes show clear fluorescence signals in the blue and red channels. For exogenous exposure of Zn2+ in the zebrafishes, after incubating the N,S-CDs-loaded zebrafishes with 30 μM Zn2+ for 30 min, its fluorescence imaging was shown in Fig. 3 A c1-c4. It can be seen from Fig. 3 B that compared with the zebrafishes which only the probe was added, the blue fluorescence intensity of the zebrafishes hardly changes, while the red channel fluorescence intensity has some decrease, suggesting that the exposure of Zn2+ can quench the red fluorescence of the probe in the zebrafishes. For exogenous exposure of HOCl in the zebrafishes, the blue channel fluorescence signal is significantly attenuated and the red channel fluorescence signal has no change, so the fluorescence color of the merged images changes from purple to red. In addition, since HOCl is one of the important ROS biomarkers in the inflammatory cells, LPS (10 mg/L) was selected to induce the zebrafishes to build an oxidative stress model in order to achieve fluorescence imaging of endogenous HOCl in the zebrafishes. As shown in Fig. 3 A d2, the fluorescence signals in the blue channel are significantly decreased due to the endogenous HOCl increase in the LPS-stimulated zebrafishes. To verify that such change is caused by endogenous HOCl, the original LPS-induced zebrafishes were further treated with 200.0 μM NAC, which is the scavenger of active oxygen. As shown in Fig. 3 A e1-e4, the internal fluorescence in the blue channels was partially recovered, suggesting that the endogenous HOCl is indeed the main cause that results in the blue fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs. Thus, the dual-emission probe can real-time display the dynamic changes of internal HOCl and Zn2+ concentrations through the blue and red channels. As a sensitive and non-invasive tool to monitor HOCl and Zn2+ fluctuations in the zebrafishes, it can lay a technical foundation for further understanding the interaction mechanism between oxidative stress and inflammation in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing the HOCl Levels in the zebrafishes under the exposure of Cr2O7 2- and TBBP-A
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the effects of pollutants on oxidative stress in vivo, the zebrafish as a classic model for exploring various exposure effects such as biocompatibility, toxicity, teratogenicity and immune response was selected in this study [37]. As shown in Fig. 4, the fluorescence signal or the mean fluorescence intensities of the blue channels in the zebrafish gradually decrease with the increase of exposure concentrations of Cr2O7 2- and TBBP-A, indicating that the concentration of HOCl gradually increases. The internal HOCl yield is dose-dependent for the two pollutants. Moreover, at the low concentration, the internal fluorescence intensity is more affected by TBBP-A (0.02 μΜ) than that of Cr2O7 2- (1.0 μΜ), suggesting that TBBP-A is easier to induce the oxidative stress in vivo compared with that of Cr2O7 2-. Furthermore, there is an interesting finding that in the merged images, the fluorescence is mainly concentrated in the digestive system including the liver or intestines of zebrafishes, suggesting that the inflammatory reaction may be most evident under the short-term exposure to pollutants [38], and it is rational to assume that the digestive system of zebrafishes may be the main metabolic site for metabolizing and accumulating of pollutants. In brief, all the above results indicate that the proposed probe of the N,S-CDs can effectively and real-time monitor the dynamic changes of HOCl and the oxidative stress in vivo.

In summary, a dual-emission and dual-response fluorescent probe of the N,S-CDs was synthesized through a simple and rapid microwave method. The probe has highly sensitivity and selectivity for the simultaneous determination of HOCl and Zn2+. It has been successfully applied for the simultaneous determination of HOCl and Zn2+ in the actual environmental water samples. Moreover, the proposed probe of the N,S-CDs not only can be used to perform fluorescence imaging of endogenous and exogenous HOCl and Zn2+ in the zebrafishes, but also can effectively and real-time monitor the dynamic changes of HOCl and the oxidative stress in the zebrafishes. Therefore, the probe will have a potential application prospect for environmental monitoring and evaluating the health risks of environmental pollutants to organisms.

Meng Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Changlun Tong: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176168) and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LZ20B070001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135617.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Meng Zhang is a PhD candidate in Environmental Science at Zhejiang University, China. She received her BS degree in Environmental Science from University of Jinan, Jinan, China, in 2019. Her research interests are environmental analytical chemistry.

Changlun Tong received his PhD in Environmental Science from Zhejiang University in 2005. He has been a professor of Environmental Science at Zhejiang University since 2011. His research interests are environmental analytical chemistry, in particular fluorescent probe and fluorescent imaging, sample pretreatment.
